data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was driven by a systemic fragility in the palatial redistribution economy where centralized storage and specialized labor created a single point of failure.", "mechanism": "Positive feedback loop of centralization: as palaces absorbed more surplus to support specialists, local resilience dropped; when a shock hit, the inability of local nodes to self-organize caused cascading institutional failure.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "Phoenician coastal enclaves"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of robust, independent local grain storage or non-palatial craft production persisting through the transition period in the core collapse zones.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Aligns with the systemic fragility hypothesis (artifact 576914b9), emphasizing internal structural weakness over external shock."}
